content: Welcome to Ionic Stacks: A mechanical engineer’s first-principles journey to demystify electrochemical climate tech, brutally confronting the greatest scaling challenges and criticisms today.Electrochemistry uses renewable electricity to unlock reactions that would otherwise only occur at tremendously high temperatures and pressures. It is a powerful tool to tackle the climate crisis, from decarbonizing the air and oceans to providing an alternative pathway for sustainable fuel and mineral production.Direct Air CaptureMarine Carbon Dioxide RemovalPower-to-XMinerals
title: Electrochemical Climate Tech | Ionic Stacks

summary: Investigating the land, time, and active intervention needed for reforestation.
title: Why don't we just plant more trees?
Marine Carbon Dioxide Removal (MCDR) Deep Dive

content:
Seawater composition#Synthetic seawater is a great laboratory tool for studying marine-based technologies. However, the ocean is a living electrolyte, and not so forgiving. Even after we take out the fish, seaweed, and sand, we are still left with a complex mix of ions. The table below summarizes the major constituents of seawater, adapted from Sverdrup and Armbrust1:
Component Approx. Concentration (mM) Chloride (Cl-) ~560 Sodium (Na+) ~481 Magnesium (Mg2+) ~54 Sulfate (SO42-) ~29 Calcium (Ca2+) ~11 Potassium (K+) ~10 Bicarbonate (HCO3-) ~2 Only ~2 mM of DIC#Challenge: From the chemical inventory above, we can immediately see the primary bottleneck of mCDR: the dilute concentration of DIC (primarily in the form of bicarbonate). In an ideal system, ~11,360 tonnes of seawater needs to be processed to extract 1 tonne of CO2. In real systems, the volume of water required increases based on the target pH of the electrochemical stack (which determines CO2 speciation based on the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation), and the time allowed for CO2 degassing (which is governed by Fick’s Law of Diffusion and two-film boundary layer equations). The electrical energy required for the intake pumps can quickly add up if large volumes of water need to be pumped over a steep pressure drop (\(\Delta P\)).
Try the slider below to contextualize how much water we would have to process to extract 1 tonne of CO2. The estimation assumes a composite acid dissociation constant (\(pK_a\)) of 5.842, which considers the dehydration of carbonic acid and seawater salinity, and a volumetric mass transfer coefficient (\(k_La\)) of 0.045 s-1, which is representative of an optimized industrial-scale degasser.
Seawater needed per tonne CO2Electrochemical stack target pH: 5.0 (max extractable CO2: 87%) CO2 degassing time: 30 s (actual CO2 yield: 65%) Seawater Processed
17,557tonnes
(~7 Olympic swimming pools)Solution: To reduce the energy requirement of intake pumps, mCDR systems can minimize the \(\Delta P\) through wide, shallow, open-channel flow designs. The electrochemical stack can also be suspended in a flowing water stream, leveraging the power of gravitational and tidal forces for passive pumping. Although this design entirely eliminates the mechanical pumps, it introduces variability in the flow rate and stricter material requirements.
Microbes and particulates#Challenge: Electrolyzers are notoriously sensitive to solid matter. Microorganisms and suspended particulates will not only clog narrow electrolyte flow channels, but also foul the membranes and electrodes, rapidly degrading stack performance. The immediate engineering response is, of course, filtration. However, the pumping energy scales directly with the volume of water processed and the pressure drop of the filters required to achieve high purity.
Solution: Fortunately, many existing coastal facilities already have high water purification standards. By integrating mCDR stacks directly downstream of desalination plants or once-through power plant cooling systems, the pumping and filtration costs are significantly reduced. Alternatively, the stack design can be made robust enough to survive the biological load through periodic cleaning. This includes hydraulic backwashing and periodic polarity reversal (PPR) which dissolves organic matter through localized pH swings. However, this approach sacrifices total system uptime and increase hardware complexity.
Chlorine evolution#Challenge: Going back to the seawater composition table again, we see that Cl- is the number 1 ion floating in the ocean. On the anode, Cl- can oxidize and evolve into toxic chlorine gas (Cl2) or bleach (ClO-), posing a threat to both the long-term operation of the electrochemical stack and the marine ecosystem. The chlorine evolution reaction (ClER) diverts anodic current away from the target reaction, which is to produce protons and generate an acidic stream for either CO2 extraction or valorization, reducing the energy efficiency of the overall process.
Solution: Chlorine generation can be suppressed by altering the reaction kinetics and thermodynamics at the anode, restricting the local mass transfer of Cl- at the boundary layer, or flowing seawater in separate compartments to physically isolate the anode3.
Kinetics and thermodynamics: The thermodynamic potential for ClER is close to the target proton-producing oxygen evolution reaction (OER):
\( \text{OER:} \quad H_2O \rightarrow \frac{1}{2} O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- (E^ \circ = 1.23 \, \text{V vs. SHE})\)
\( \text{ClER:} \quad 2Cl^- \rightarrow Cl_2 + 2e^- (E^ \circ = 1.36 \, \text{V vs. SHE})\)
We can simultaneously lower the activation energy for OER and increase the kinetics barrier for ClER by designing selective catalysts. Increasing the local anode pH can further promote OER over ClER. Alternatively, we can switch to anode reactions that are thermodynamically more favourable, constraining the anodic potential to prevent chlorine evolution. One example is the hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR), which can proceed at a much lower voltage but introduces complexities in gas handling and material selection:
\( \text{HOR:} \quad H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^- (E^ \circ = 0.00 \, \text{V vs. SHE})\)
Mass transfer: This approach focuses on keeping the Cl- away from the anode by altering the local reaction environment. Coatings and adlayers can act as molecular sieves that use steric hindrance and electrostatic repulsion to reduce Cl- concentration at the active sites.
Stack design: Sometimes an architectural overhaul is exactly what we need. Instead of feeding seawater to the anode compartment, we can use membranes to create physical barriers and confine the seawater to central flow channels. One example of this is the bipolar membrane electrodialysis stack. This approach achieves near-complete Cl- isolation (dependent on the permselectivity of the membranes) but also increases the stack’s ohmic resistance.
Magnesium and calcium precipitates#Challenge: Going back to the seawater composition table one last time, we see that Mg2+ and Ca2+ also exist in significant quantities. As the cathode generates OH- to produce the target alkaline stream, the localized high-pH environment precipitates Mg2+ as Mg(OH)2 and shifts the bicarbonate equilibrium to precipitate Ca2+ as CaCO3. These precipitates passivate the catalyst, clog the flow channels, and rupture the ion-exchange membranes.
Solution: Similar to the fight against chlorine generation, seawater can be confined to central flow channels to keep Mg2+ and Ca2+ away from the cathode’s localized alkaline environment. An alternative, operationally driven, head-on approach continuously removes the mineral scale through frequent polarity reversals, periodic solubility shifts, and raw hydrodynamic forces.
References#Sverdrup, K. A. & Armbrust, E. V. An Introduction to the World’s Oceans 10th edn (McGraw-Hill, 2008). ↩︎
Millero, F. J., Graham, T. B., Huang, F., Bustos-Serrano, H. & Pierrot, D. Dissociation constants of carbonic acid in seawater as a function of salinity and temperature. Mar. Chem. 100, 80-94 (2006). https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marchem.2005.12.001 ↩︎
Bahuguna, G. & Patolsky, F. Routes to Avoiding Chlorine Evolution in Seawater Electrolysis: Recent Perspective and Future Directions. ACS Materials Lett. 6, 3202-3217 (2024). https://doi.org/10.1021/acsmaterialslett.4c00409 ↩︎
summary: Challenges and solutions to working with real seawater in electrolyzers
title: When electrochemistry meets the open seas

content:
The air around us contains an average of 425 ppm (0.0425 vol%) of CO2. This dilute concentration is precisely why DAC represents one of the greatest engineering challenges in climate tech: it is an absolute uphill battle against thermodynamics. While conventional carbon capture relies on heat for the regeneration process, electrochemistry can drive the separation using electrons, allowing the system to operate entirely on a renewable grid.
pH-Swing Architecture#The behaviour of CO2 is determined by the acidity (pH) of its environment. In an alkaline solvent, CO2 is captured and held in a stable, dissolved state (as bicarbonate, carbonate, or carbamate ions). The electrochemical stack uses electricity to simultaneously generate acidic and alkaline conditions to shift this balance. The acid is used to release a CO2 as a concentrated gas, while the base regenerates the capture solvent so that it can loop back to the air contactor and capture more CO2.
flowchart LR%% define the nodesAmbient[/"Ambient Air"/]Contactor["Air Contactor"]Clean[/"CO₂-Clean Air"/]Pure[/"High-Purity CO₂"/]Stack[["Stack"]]%% define the flowAmbient --> ContactorContactor --> CleanContactor --> |CO₂-Rich| StackStack --> |CO₂-Lean| ContactorStack --> Pure%% stylingclassDef StackStyle padding:25px 50px,stroke-width:4px,font-weight:bold,font-size:18px;class Stack StackStyleElectro-Swing Architecture#These electrodes are like “switchable CO2 magnets.” Rather than circulating liquid electrolytes, this architecture relies on redox-active electrodes that change their chemical affinity based on the applied voltage. The system captures CO2 while in a reduced state and releases it in an oxidized state when the polarity is reversed.
flowchart LR%% define the nodesAmbient[/"Ambient Air"/]Clean[/"CO₂-Clean Air"/]Stack[["Stack"]]Pure[/"High-Purity CO₂"/]%% define the flowAmbient --> StackStack --> CleanStack --> Pure%% stylingclassDef StackStyle padding:25px 50px,stroke-width:4px,font-weight:bold,font-size:18px;class Stack StackStyleSifting the SkiesWhy don't we just plant more trees?April 28 2026·4 minsHow much energy do we actually need to pull CO2 out of thin air?April 6 2024·5 mins
title: Direct Air Capture (DAC)

content:
The central repository for technical deep dives, perspectives, and peer-reviewed publications.
Direct Air Capture (DAC) Deep Dive→How much energy do we actually need to pull CO2 out of thin air?Direct Air Capture (DAC) Perspective→Why don’t we just plant more trees?Marine Carbon Dioxide Removal (mCDR) Deep Dive→When electrochemistry meets the open seasPeer-Reviewed Publications#[24] Efficient acidic CO2 electrolysis with suppressed crossover in a separator-based membrane electrode assembly#Liu et al., Journal of American Chemical Society, 2026
A zero-gap membrane electrode assembly incorporating an ∼100 μm electrolyte-filled hydrophilic porous separator regulates coupled H+ and K+ transport to promotes C2+ production while suppressing crossover.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[23] Solvent tuning regulates proton flux to extend stability in reactive CO2 capture and electrolysis#Xiao et al., Chem Catalysis, 2026
The degradation of amino-acid-based reactive CO2 capture was dominated by processes localized at the catalytic interface, where high proton flux accelerates catalyst decomposition. By regulating the cathode’s local pH through capture solvent tuning and operating parameter optimization, the stability was extended to over 75 hours at 100 mA cm−2 and over 250 hours at 50 mA cm−2.. Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[22] Electrosynthesis of ethylene from syngas#Li et al., Nature Sustainability, 2026
A COOH-functionalized active carbon material, which captures CO from syngas, strengthens CO adsorption on a copper cathode and enhances ethylene FE even under CO-diluted conditions.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[21] Industrial amine blends enable efficient CO electrosynthesis in reactive capture#Sun et al., EES Catalysis, 2026
Optimizing solvents for reactive CO2 capture requires balancing high bicarbonate concentrations against the increased viscosity and resistance that can hinder CO2 transport and conversion.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[20] Accelerated discovery of CO2-to-C3-hydrocarbon electrocatalysts with human-in-the-loop#Kim et al., Joule, 2025
A machine learning algorithm that integrates human domain knowledge for real-time feature editing, coupled with an accelerated experimental platform, achieved a ~165× acceleration in discovering multi-element CO2 electrocatalysts.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[19] Efficient amino-acid-based reactive capture of CO2 via nickel molecular catalyst#Guo et al., Nature Communications, 2025
A nickel molecular catalyst supported on carbon nanotubes with a conjugated NiPc framework resists amino acid adsorption and promotes CO2 adsorption to enhances CO selectivity in a reactive capture system.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[18] CO electrolysers with 51% energy efficiency towards C2+ using porous separators#Miao et al., Nature Energy, 2025
An uncharged porous separator enables facile transport of both ion types, reducing ohmic resistance and superconcentrating cations at the catalyst surface, leading to a full-cell voltage of 1.95 V at 200 mA cm−2 and an energy efficiency of 51% to C2+ products sustained over 250 hours.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[17] High-nuclearity copper molecular catalysts for electrocatalytic CO-to-acetate conversion#Bodiuzzaman et al., Journal of the American Chemical Society, 2025
Efficient electrosynthesis of acetate via CO electroreduction on Cu NC-based catalysts enabled by tuning the local hydrophobicity through ligand adjustment, and balancing the ligand coverage and active site exposure.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[16] Scaled CO electroreduction to alcohols#Papangelakis et al., Nature Communications, 2025
An 800 cm2 CO electrolyzer yielded 200 mL of alcohol/day by reversing water’s electroosmotic drag via a cation exchange membrane assembly and employing a multi-step condenser.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[15] A redox-active polymeric network facilitates electrified reactive-capture electrosynthesis to multi-carbon products from dilute CO2-containing streams#Zhang et al., Nature Communications, 2025
A redox-active polymeric network whose polymer fragments undergo reversible reduction during the electrochemical conversion process, enables electron transfer to CO2 molecules generated in-situ from carbonate capture liquid.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[14] Rapid screening of CO2 capture fluids#Guo et al., Lab on a Chip, 2025
A microfluidic approach paired with automated image processing and density functional theory simulations that enables comprehensive rapid screening of CO2 capture fluids.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[13] Scalability and stability in CO2 reduction via tomography-guided system design#O'Brien et al., Joule, 2024
A GDE architecture that is resistant to flooding and maintains stable CO2 electrolysis performance for >400 hours due to a percolating network of polytetrafluoroethylene in the microporous layer that retains hydrophobicity.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[12] Reactive capture of CO2 via amino acid#Xiao et al., Nature Communications, 2024
An amino-acid-based reactive CO2 capture system increased CO selectivity by employing a single-atom catalyst, engineering the capture solution, and elevating the operating temperature and pressure to the increase the availability of in-situ CO2.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[11] Improving the SO2 tolerance of CO2 reduction electrocatalysts using a polymer/catalyst/ionomer heterojunction design#Papangelakis et al., Nature Energy, 2024
A polymer/catalyst/ionomer heterojunction design with combined hydrophobic and highly charged hydrophilic domains that diminish hydrogen adsorption and promote CO2 over SO2 transport.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[10] Direct air capture of CO2 via cyclic viologen electrocatalysis#Liu et al., Energy & Environmental Science, 2024
An electrochemical process to regenerate hydroxide absorbents via cyclic viologen electrocatalysis by isolating the redox-active viologens to avoid oxidative degradation and vaporization loss, and tuning the viologen substituent to facilitate fast reaction kinetics.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[9] Scale-dependent techno-economic analysis of CO2 capture and electroreduction to ethylene#Alerte et al., ACS Sustainable Chemistry & Engineering, 2023
A techno-economic assessment of electrochemically converting CO2 from an industrial point source to polymer-grade ethylene using industrially mature upstream and downstream separation technologies in an Aspen Plus model.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[8] Electrified cement production via anion-mediated electrochemical calcium extraction#Miao et al., ACS Energy Letters, 2023
Electrochemical cement production based on anion-mediated electrochemical calcium extraction that uses situ-generated acidic electrolytes to release the feedstock CO2 emissions as a high-purity gas stream.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[7] Regeneration of direct air CO2 capture liquid via alternating electrocatalysis#Xu et al., Joule, 2023
A low-temperature electrochemical process to regenerate alkaline capture liquids via alternating electrocatalysis by colocating oxidation and reduction reactions on a single electrode and cycling between electrolyzer and fuel cell modes.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[6] Cationic-group-functionalized electrocatalysts enable stable acidic CO2 electrolysis#Fan et al., Nature Catalysis, 2023
A copper catalyst functionalized with immobilized benzimidazolium cationic groups enable efficient CO2 activation and over 150 hours of stable CO2 electrolysis under acidic conditions.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[5] CO2 electroreduction to multicarbon products from carbonate capture liquid#Lee et al., Joule, 2023
Direct electrochemical conversion of CO2, captured in the form of carbonate, into multicarbon (C2+) products using an interposer and a Cu/CoPc-CNTs electrocatalyst.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[4] Single-site decorated copper enables energy- and carbon-efficient CO2 methanation in acidic conditions#Fan et al., Nature Communications, 2023
Hexadentate donor sites in ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid enable the chelation of Cu ions, regulating Cu cluster size and forming Cu-N/O single sites that achieve high CH4 selectivity in acidic CO2 electrolysis.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[3] Pilot-scale CO2 electrolysis enables a semi-empirical electrolyzer model#Edwards et al., ACS Energy Letters, 2023
A semi-empirical electrolyzer model built in Aspen Custom Modeler, trained on a 5 cm2 lab-scale CO2 electrolyzer and validated with data from a pilot-scale 800 cm2 single cell and a 10 × 800 cm2 stack.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[2] Direct carbonate electrolysis into pure syngas#Xiao et al., EES Catalysis, 2023
A composite CO2 diffusion layer attains high CO selectivity by achieving high alkalinity and in-situ CO2 concentration at the cathode of a reactive CO2 capture electrolyzer.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
[1] High-throughput parallelized testing of membrane electrode assemblies of CO2 reduction#Kose et al., Catalysis Science & Technology, 2022
An accelerated, parallelized membrane electrode assembly testing system that enables uniform compression, improving repeatability and reliability.
Article (PDF)  ·  Supplemental Information (PDF)
title: Technical Index

content:
The ocean is a massive carbon sink. Covering ~71% of the Earth’s surface, it acts as the world’s largest air contactor that absorbs CO2 from the atmosphere in an attempt to maintain an equilibrium. Ocean surface water contains on average ~2 mM of dissolved inorganic carbon (DIC)—CO2 in its various chemical states, including aqueous CO2, carbonic acid, bicarbonate, and carbonate ions. Excess CO2 acidifies the ocean, pushing this natural buffer to its limits and threatening marine ecosystems. The goal of mCDR is to restore this delicate balance by either extracting the dissolved carbon or permanently expanding the ocean’s safe absorption capacity.
Direct Ocean Capture (DOC)#Electrochemical DOC relies on the ocean’s natural carbonate equilibrium. The electrochemical stack uses electricity to split the incoming seawater to generate acids and bases. When the seawater pH is shifted down using the acidic stream, the DIC is extracted as a concentrated CO2 gas stream. The DIC-depleted water is then recombined with the alkaline stream to rebalance the pH and returned to the ocean.
flowchart LR%% define the nodesIntake[/"Intake Seawater"/]Stack[["Stack"]]Pure[/"High-Purity CO₂"/]Outflow[/"Outflow Seawater"/]%% define the flowIntake -->|DIC-Rich| StackStack --> PureStack -->|DIC-Depleted| Outflow%% stylingclassDef StackStyle padding:25px 50px,stroke-width:4px,font-weight:bold,font-size:18px;class Stack StackStyleOcean Alkalinity Enhancement (OAE)#OAE doesn’t involve any CO2 handling. Instead, this approach focuses on expanding the ocean’s CO2 absorption capacity. The electrochemical stack splits seawater or waste brine into an alkaline stream for ocean discharge and an acidic stream for use in other processes. The added alkalinity shifts the carbonate equilibrium in the ocean, converting dissolved aqueous CO2 into bicarbonates, and restoring the ocean’s natural buffering capacity.
flowchart LR%% define the nodesIntake[/"Intake Seawater / Brine"/]Stack[["Stack"]]Outflow[/"Outflow Seawater"/]Acid[/"Acid Valorization"/]%% define the flowIntake --> StackStack --> |Acidic Stream|AcidStack --> |Alkaline Stream|Outflow%% stylingclassDef StackStyle padding:25px 50px,stroke-width:4px,font-weight:bold,font-size:18px;class Stack StackStyleScrubbing the SeasWhen electrochemistry meets the open seasApril 11 2026·6 mins
title: Marine Carbon Dioxide Removal (mCDR)

content:
Critical minerals, such as lithium, nickel, cobalt, and rare earth elements, are essential components in modern batteries and high-performance electronics. These industries are resource-intensive, but they are also the powerhouses of the global energy transition. However, extracting these minerals from the Earth’s crust has become increasingly difficult and expensive as high-grade ore deposits are depleted. Electrochemistry offers a sustainable pathway to extract and recover these minerals from unconventional sources, including brines, industrial wastewater, and spent batteries.
Direct Lithium Extraction (DLE)#Electrochemical DLE relies on electro-swing adsorption. It uses electrical potentials to drive lithium ions (Li+) into selective, redox-active electrodes. Once the electrode reaches capacity, the polarity is reversed to release the Li+ into a release electrolyte. This enriched stream is then concentrated and the lithium is precipitated as high-purity solids.
flowchart LR%% define the nodesBrine[/"Brine"/]Release[/"Release Electrolyte"/]Stack[["Stack"]]Outflow[/"Concentrated Li⁺ Solution"/]Spent[/"Spent Brine"/]Pure[/"Lithium Solids"/]%% define the flowBrine --> StackRelease --> StackStack --> OutflowStack --> SpentOutflow --> Pure%% stylingclassDef StackStyle padding:25px 50px,stroke-width:4px,font-weight:bold,font-size:18px;class Stack StackStyleElectrochemical Salt Splitting#The electrochemical stack splits salt solutions into concentrated acidic and alkaline streams. The acid is used to leach target minerals from secondary feedstocks, such as battery black mass or mining tailings, while the base is used to selectively precipitate these minerals as solids. After the mineral recovery process, the resulting neutralized salt solution is returned to the stack, closing the loop.
flowchart LR%% define the nodesSalt[/"Salt Solution"/]Stack[["Stack"]]Recovery["Acid Leaching & Base Precipitation"]Pure[/"Mineral Solids"/]%% define the flowSalt --> StackStack --> |Acidic Stream|RecoveryStack --> |Alkaline Stream|RecoveryRecovery --> PureRecovery --> Salt%% stylingclassDef StackStyle padding:25px 50px,stroke-width:4px,font-weight:bold,font-size:18px;class Stack StackStyle
title: Minerals

content:
Our world today is built on a foundation of chemicals and fuels. Traditionally, these products originate from fossil feedstocks and are synthesized in energy-intensive, thermochemical reactors operating at high temperatures and pressures. Power-to-X (PtX) answers the question: How do we sustainably meet the energy and material demands of a modernizing world? From feedstocks to energy inputs, PtX is the shift from thermochemical to electrochemical systems that rely on water, captured CO2, and renewable electricity. PtX plays a key part in broad decarbonization efforts, producing eChemicals and eFuels for hard-to-abate sectors, such as aviation, shipping, steel production, and chemical manufacturing, where high energy density remains essential.
flowchart LR%% define the nodesWater[/"Water (H₂O)"/]CO2[/"Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)"/]Stack[["Stack"]]Hydrogen[/"Hydrogen (H₂)"/]Syngas[/"Syngas (H₂ + CO)"/]Cprod[/"Carbon-Based Products"/]%% define the flowWater --> StackCO2 --> StackStack --> HydrogenStack --> SyngasStack --> Cprod%% stylingclassDef StackStyle padding:25px 50px,stroke-width:4px,font-weight:bold,font-size:18px;class Stack StackStyleGreen Hydrogen (H2)#The most mature “X” is H2. In an electrochemical stack, H2 is generated at the cathode through the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER):
\(2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2\)H2 is a versatile and powerful molecule. It can be used directly as a fuel for heavy-duty transport or as a reducing agent in steel manufacturing to replace carbon-heavy coke. It is also an essential feedstock for industrial processes such as green ammonia production, where H2 is combined with atmospheric nitrogen to synthesize sustainable fertilizers via the Haber-Bosch process.
Carbon Dioxide (CO2) Valorization#CO2 electrolysis uses electricity to “reverse combustion” through the CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR). The most industrially viable product is carbon monoxide (CO), which can be combined with green H2 to form synthesis gas (syngas) and used as a feedstock in the Fischer-Tropsch process to produce sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) or synthetic diesel.
\(CO_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow CO + H_2O\)CO2 electrolysis can also generate multi-carbon (C2+) products such as ethylene (C2H4), a building block for the global plastics industry, and ethanol (C2H5OH), a high-energy density fuel additive, versatile industrial solvent, and ingredient of alcoholic beverages.
\(2CO_2 + 12H^+ + 12e^- \rightarrow C_2H_4 + 4H_2O\)\(2CO_2 + 12H^+ + 12e^- \rightarrow C_2H_5OH + 3H_2O\)
Sparking the Stock
title: Power-to-X (PtX)

content:
The minimum theoretical work#There’s nothing engineers love more than energy-efficient systems. In a perfect system, 100% of the energy input performs useful work. When it comes to direct air capture, useful work means gathering dispersed CO2 molecules from the air into a concentrated gas stream. This process decreases entropy and is non-spontaneous. Thus, there is a thermodynamic minimum work of separation (\(W_{ \text{min}}\)), the absolute energy floor for a reversible, 100% efficient process.
To calculate \(W_{ \text{min}}\), we turn to the Gibbs free energy of mixing (\( \Delta G_{ \text{mix}}\)). We assume constant temperature and pressure, and treat air as an ideal gas where the enthalpy of mixing is zero (\(\Delta H_{ \text{mix}}=0\)). Gibbs free energy is derived from the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics and represents the work needed to overcome the tendency of molecules to disperse and mix (some statistical mechanics required).
\(W_{ \text{min}}=- \Delta G_{ \text{mix}}=-nRT \sum x_i \ln(x_i)\) Where:
\(n\) is the number of moles \(R\) is the universal ideal gas constant (\(8.314 \, \text{J}/( \text{mol} \cdot \text{K})\)) \(T\) is the temperature (\(298.15 \text{ K}\)) \(x_i\) is the mole fraction of gas \(i\) (\(x_1\) is \(\text{CO}_2\) and \(x_2\) is everything else) Note the negative sign here! \(\Delta G_{ \text{mix}}\) itself is negative because mixing is a spontaneous process, and \(W_{ \text{min}}\) will be positive because we have to put in work. Sprinkle in some algebra to normalize the equation for 1 mole of CO2. The negative sign then cancels out due to a log identity. The equation becomes:
\(W_{ \text{min}}=RT \left[ \ln \left( \dfrac{1}{x_ \text{in}} \right)+ \dfrac{1-{x_ \text{in}}}{x_ \text{in}} \ln \left( \dfrac{1}{1-{x_ \text{in}}} \right) \right]\) Where:
\(x_ \text{in}\) is the concentration of \(\text{CO}_2\) flowing into the system (\(425 \text{ ppm}\)) This equation can be further simplified since \(x_ \text{in}\) is very small:
\(W_{ \text{min}} \approx RT \left[ \ln \left( \dfrac{1}{x_ \text{in}} \right)+ 1 \right]\) When we plug in our ambient conditions, we find the minimum required energy for direct air capture, before a single fan, pump, heater, or power supply has been turned on:
\(W_{ \text{min}} \approx \mathbf{21.7 \, \text{kJ/mol}} \approx \mathbf{0.49 \, \text{GJ/t}_{\text{CO}_2}} \approx \mathbf{137 \, \text{kWh/t}_{\text{CO}_2}}\) Real systems are hard#Real systems involve irreversibilities (e.g. fluid friction, thermal gradients, and kinetic overpotentials) that increase the actual energy requirement. Let’s take Carbon Engineering’s direct air capture using an aqueous potassium hydroxide sorbent as an example. Their pilot plant data was published in 20181.
The first challenge: moving massive volumes of air through a sorbent interface.
CO2 is very dilute in the atmosphere and capturing 1 tonne of CO2 requires processing ~2.62 million m3 of air (\(V_{ \text{air}}\)), assuming a 50% capture efficiency. These CO2 capture units, or air contactors, use structured packing with high surface-area-to-volume ratios to maximize the reaction interface. Imagine, perhaps, a tightly layered corrugated cardboard structure.
High-powered fans are needed to overcome the pressure drop (\( \Delta P\)) and push the air through. The specific fan energy (\(E_{\text{fan}}\)) can be calculated using the following equation, assuming a \( \Delta P\) of 100 Pa and fan efficiency (\(\eta_{\text{fan}}\)) of 70%:
\(E_{\text{fan}} = \dfrac{V_{ \text{air}} \Delta P}{ \eta_{\text{fan}}}\) The capture efficiency and \( \Delta P\) assumptions are slightly more conservative than the reported numbers. Nevertheless, they provide a reasonable baseline for an average air contactor:
\(E_{\text{fan}} \approx \mathbf{0.37 \, \text{GJ/t}_{\text{CO}_2}} \approx \mathbf{104 \, \text{kWh/t}_{\text{CO}_2}}\) The second (perhaps much more daunting) challenge: separating CO2 from the sorbent.
Sorbent selection is sticky business. A delicate balance needs to be achieved: strong-binding sorbents have fast reaction kinetics and can efficiently remove CO2 from air, but if the binding is too strong, massive amounts of energy are required to release it. Conventional thermal-swing processes use high-grade heat to break these chemical bonds and regenerate the sorbent. In real systems, the applied heat is much more than the theoretical energy of the bond because it has to account for heating up the CO2 and everything around it, while overcoming the activation energy barrier.
In the Carbon Engineering process, a precipitation step was added after the air contactor to reduce the energy wasted on water evaporation. Even so, a good chunk of thermal energy was used for sorbent regeneration at 900°C:
\(E_{\text{regen}} \approx \mathbf{5.25 \, \text{GJ/t}_{\text{CO}_2}} \approx \mathbf{1458 \, \text{kWh/t}_{\text{CO}_2}}\) Minor loads still add up
Beyond the main fans and sorbent regenerator, the plant also needs energy for supporting equipment. These can include fluid pumps, CO2 compressors, air separation units, and auxiliary equipment.
Just here for the table (TLDR)#The total energy needed to pull CO2 out of thin air varies significantly across the industry, driven by 3 key factors:
Capture chemistry: liquid absorption, solid adsorption, or physisorption Sorbent type: alkali hydroxides, amine-functionalized solids, or porous membranes Regeneration mode: thermal or electrochemical Below is a summary of the energy consumption reported by select companies, adapted from Wang et al2.
Company Technology Total energy consumption (\(\text{GJ/t}_{\text{CO}_2}\)) / (\(\text{kWh/t}_{\text{CO}_2}\)) Energy efficiency (%) Carbon Engineering Thermal-swing absorption 6.3–7.2 / 1,750–2,000 7–8% Climeworks Temperature-vacuum swing adsorption 6.3–7.2 / 1,750–2,000 7–8% Mission Zero pH-swing absorption 1.4–2.3 / 400–650 21–34% Electrochemical direct air capture#While there are many ways to reduce energy consumption, one way forward is turning to electrochemistry. These systems use different sorbent regeneration mechanisms that more precisely target the CO2-sorbent bond, eliminating the bulk-heating requirements of conventional processes entirely.
As the global energy landscape evolves, the industrial deployment of direct air capture must align with the availability of excess renewable electricity. Electrochemical systems are fully electrified and can be strategically installed in remote regions with ample renewable capacity. Modular and flexible, they are a strong contender for scalable and sustainable carbon removal technology.

summary: Technical foundations on minimum work, actual work, and second law efficiencies for direct air capture.
title: How much energy do we actually need to pull CO<sub>2</sub> out of thin air?
